content.text: — they live inside a host application and provide context-aware suggestions, completions, or answers without the user leaving their workflow. The key difference from standalone chatbots is that a copilot has access to
: the file you're editing, the ticket you're viewing, the code you just highlighted, the customer record on screen. That context is the raw material for useful, grounded suggestions.

attrs.alt: Copilot pattern: context injection and inline suggestion

content.text: The term "assistant" is often used loosely. The meaningful architectural distinction is whether the AI reads
(copilot) or waits for the user to paste context (chatbot).
The copilot's value comes from high-quality context. The host application SDK or extension is responsible for extracting and ranking the most relevant signals:

# Copilot context extraction — gather relevant context from the host app
# This runs inside your IDE extension / app SDK before each LLM call.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class CopilotContext:
    # Primary signal: what the user is looking at
    active_file_content: str
    cursor_position: tuple[int, int]   # (line, col)
    selected_text: str | None

    # Secondary signals: related files
    open_tabs: list[tuple[str, str]]   # [(filename, content), ...]
    recent_edits: list[str]            # last N changed lines

    # User intent
    instruction: str

    # Tool outputs (optional, from previous tool calls)
    linter_output: str | None = None
    test_output: str | None = None

def extract_context(editor_state: dict) -> CopilotContext:
    """Extract and rank context from the editor state."""
    active = editor_state["active_file"]
    cursor = editor_state["cursor"]

    # Window the active file around the cursor — don't send the whole file
    lines = active["content"].splitlines()
    start = max(0, cursor[0] - 50)
    end   = min(len(lines), cursor[0] + 50)
    windowed = "\n".join(lines[start:end])

    # Limit open tabs to the most recently used (MRU) — avoid token overflow
    mru_tabs = sorted(editor_state["open_tabs"], key=lambda t: t["last_access"], reverse=True)
    tab_snippets = [(t["name"], t["content"][:500]) for t in mru_tabs[:5]]

    return CopilotContext(
        active_file_content=windowed,
        cursor_position=cursor,
        selected_text=editor_state.get("selection"),
        open_tabs=tab_snippets,
        recent_edits=editor_state.get("recent_edits", [])[-10:],
        instruction=editor_state["user_instruction"],
    )
send the lines around the cursor, not the entire file — a 100-line window is usually sufficient and avoids filling the context with irrelevant code
send the most recently accessed related files first; they're more likely to be relevant than alphabetically first tabs
linter errors, test failures, and search results are high-signal context — if available, include them
truncate each context piece to a maximum token budget so no single source dominates
The prompt builder assembles extracted context into the message list, ordered by relevance:

def build_copilot_prompt(ctx: CopilotContext) -> list[dict]:
    """Assemble the message list from extracted context."""
    system = """You are a coding copilot embedded in an IDE.
Your role is to complete or improve the code at the user's cursor position.
Rules:
- Output ONLY the code that should replace the cursor position (no explanation unless asked).
- Preserve the surrounding code style, indentation, and naming conventions.
- Do not repeat code that already exists above or below the cursor.
- If the user asks a question, answer concisely in a comment or brief prose."""

    # Build context block — most relevant first to survive truncation
    context_parts = [f"# Active file (around cursor):\n{ctx.active_file_content}"]

    if ctx.selected_text:
        context_parts.append(f"# Selected text:\n{ctx.selected_text}")

    for name, content in ctx.open_tabs:
        context_parts.append(f"# Related file: {name}\n{content}")

    if ctx.linter_output:
        context_parts.append(f"# Linter output:\n{ctx.linter_output}")

    if ctx.recent_edits:
        context_parts.append(f"# Recent edits:\n" + "\n".join(ctx.recent_edits))

    user_message = "\n\n".join(context_parts) + f"\n\n# Instruction:\n{ctx.instruction}"

    return [
        {"role": "system", "content": system},
        {"role": "user", "content": user_message},
    ]
Place the most specific context (active file, selection) first — most LLMs attend more strongly to earlier content
The system prompt defines the copilot's output contract: code only, or code with explanation, or diff only
Preserve code style by including surrounding context — the model will match indentation and naming from examples it sees
Copilots must feel fast. Stream tokens to the UI as they arrive so the user sees the suggestion forming in real time ("ghost text") rather than waiting for the full response:

import asyncio
from openai import AsyncOpenAI

client = AsyncOpenAI()

async def stream_suggestion(ctx: CopilotContext) -> str:
    """Stream a copilot suggestion token by token."""
    messages = build_copilot_prompt(ctx)
    full_response = ""

    async with client.chat.completions.stream(
        model="gpt-4o",
        messages=messages,
        max_tokens=512,          # copilot suggestions should be short
        temperature=0.2,         # lower temperature for more predictable code
        stop=["\n\n\n"],         # stop at triple newline to avoid runaway generation
    ) as stream:
        async for text in stream.text_stream:
            full_response += text
            yield text   # stream token to the UI for ghost-text display

    return full_response

# In your IDE extension:
async def show_ghost_text(editor):
    ctx = extract_context(editor.state)
    buffer = ""
    async for token in stream_suggestion(ctx):
        buffer += token
        editor.show_ghost_text(buffer)   # update inline preview
    editor.commit_ghost_text(buffer)     # finalise on Tab press
code completions should be predictable, not creative
keep short — a suggestion longer than ~30 lines is usually wrong or overwhelming
stop at triple newlines or function boundaries to prevent the model from completing the entire file
trigger the LLM call only after 300–500ms of keystroke inactivity to avoid firing on every character
Copilots are not limited to code editors. Any application with structured state can embed a copilot:

# Domain-specific copilot: SQL query assistant for a data analyst tool
from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

SCHEMA = """
Tables:
- orders(id, user_id, created_at, total_usd, status)
- users(id, email, country, plan_tier)
- products(id, name, category, price_usd)
- order_items(order_id, product_id, quantity, unit_price)
"""

def sql_copilot(partial_query: str, user_question: str, query_history: list[str]) -> str:
    """Generate or complete a SQL query based on context."""
    context = f"""Database schema:
{SCHEMA}

Recent queries by this user:
{chr(10).join(f"-- {q}" for q in query_history[-3:])}

Partial query in editor:
{partial_query}"""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system="""You are a SQL copilot for a PostgreSQL analytics database.
Complete or generate SQL queries based on the schema and user intent.
Output ONLY valid SQL — no explanation, no markdown fences.
Use CTEs for complex queries. Always include a LIMIT clause for exploratory queries.""",
        messages=[
            {"role": "user", "content": f"{context}\n\nRequest: {user_question}"},
        ],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

# Example
query = sql_copilot(
    partial_query="SELECT u.country, ",
    user_question="show total revenue by country for last 30 days, top 10",
    query_history=["SELECT * FROM orders LIMIT 10", "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users"],
)
print(query)
schema + query history + partial query → complete SQL
thread history + CRM data → draft reply
component library + figma selection → CSS or Tailwind suggestion
contract type + clause library + flagged section → redline suggestion
Each domain copilot has a domain-specific system prompt, a domain-specific context extraction step, and often domain-specific output validation (e.g., parse the SQL, run the linter).
For edits with broader scope (refactoring, rewriting a function), show the user a diff before applying changes. This gives them control while still eliminating the manual work:

# Apply-and-review pattern: show a diff before making changes
from openai import OpenAI
import difflib

client = OpenAI()

def copilot_edit(original_code: str, instruction: str) -> dict:
    """
    Generate an edited version of the code, return the diff for review.
    Returns {"original": ..., "edited": ..., "diff": ..., "explanation": ...}
    """
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content":
                "You are a code editor. Return ONLY the complete edited file contents. "
                "No explanation, no markdown fences, no partial output — the full file."},
            {"role": "user", "content":
                f"File contents:\n\n{original_code}\n\nEdit instruction: {instruction}"},
        ],
    )
    edited = response.choices[0].message.content.strip()

    # Generate a unified diff for the UI to display
    diff = "\n".join(difflib.unified_diff(
        original_code.splitlines(),
        edited.splitlines(),
        fromfile="original",
        tofile="edited",
        lineterm="",
    ))

    # Separate call for the explanation (cheap, small context)
    explanation_resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[
            {"role": "user", "content":
                f"In one sentence, describe what changed:\n\n{diff}"},
        ],
    )
    return {
        "original": original_code,
        "edited": edited,
        "diff": diff,
        "explanation": explanation_resp.choices[0].message.content,
    }
Use a single model call to produce the full edited file — partial output is hard to apply reliably
Generate the diff in your code, not by the model — diff generation is deterministic and cheap
A separate, cheap model call for the one-sentence explanation is worth the cost — it helps users decide whether to accept
Present the diff in a side-by-side or unified-diff UI before writing to the file
what fraction of suggestions the user accepts (Tab press / apply) vs dismisses — the primary quality signal
how much the user edits the suggestion after accepting — a high edit distance means the suggestion was partly wrong
if users pause a long time before accepting, the suggestion may be confusing or only partially useful
how often the copilot triggers a suggestion when the user didn't want one — high false positive rates increase cognitive load
measure token overlap between accepted suggestions and the context that was injected — guides which context sources matter most
large files overflow the context window and include irrelevant code — always window around the cursor
without debouncing, each character fires an API call; debounce to 300–500ms idle time
, the model may complete the entire file instead of just the missing function
if the model can't see the surrounding code, it will use its default style (e.g., single quotes vs double quotes) which creates noisy diffs
showing partial suggestions prematurely can confuse users — wait until the first complete logical unit (closing bracket, full statement) before displaying
if the suggestion takes > 2 seconds, users start typing again — handle the race condition between new keystrokes and a pending API response
are deeply embedded AI agents that read application state automatically and provide inline suggestions — the key differentiator from chatbots
is the most impactful engineering decision: window the active file, rank related files by recency, include tool outputs
is mandatory for real-time ghost text; use low temperature and short max_tokens for predictable code completions
shows a diff before applying broader edits — gives users control without requiring manual work
Measure acceptance rate and post-acceptance edit distance
as the primary quality metrics; they directly reflect whether suggestions are useful
(SQL, email, legal, design) follow the same pattern — custom context extraction, domain-specific system prompt, domain-specific output validation
